questions.question: What is OpenClaw?

options.text: An open-source AI agent that runs locally and connects to tools via Skills

explanation.correct: OpenClaw is explicitly an open-source, locally-run AI agent that connects to external tools through Skills, not a hosted dashboard or managed service.
explanation.why: A managed dashboard implies a hosted UI, which OpenClaw isn't; it's not a paid SaaS product; it isn't an AWS Lambda runtime, it runs on the user's own machine.

questions.question: What two components make up the minimum structure of a Skill?

options.text: A Dockerfile and a YAML config
A folder and a SKILL.md file
A Lambda function and an API Gateway route
A CloudFormation template and a script

explanation.correct: A skill is one folder plus one SKILL.md file — there is no required Docker, Lambda, or CloudFormation layer.
explanation.why: Dockerfile/YAML, Lambda/API Gateway, and CloudFormation/script all describe heavier infrastructure patterns not required by OpenClaw's skill format.

questions.question: Which chat channels can OpenClaw connect to, per the session?

explanation.correct: The session names WhatsApp, Telegram, and Slack as supported channels, since OpenClaw is channel-agnostic by design.
explanation.why: WhatsApp-only, email-only, and SMS-only all undersell the channel-agnostic design described in the session.

questions.question: What does SKILL.md primarily tell the agent?

options.text: The pricing of the AWS services involved
When to trigger, what command to run, and how to handle errors
The full source code of the underlying script

explanation.correct: SKILL.md defines triggers, the invocation command, output format, and error handling for the agent to follow.
explanation.why: Pricing isn't part of a skill definition; the script's full source lives separately in scripts/; the account ID is supplied via environment variables, not SKILL.md.

questions.question: What does the agent do once a skill is triggered?

options.text: It generates an answer purely from its training data
It executes the underlying script and returns its real output
It asks the user to open the AWS console manually
It queues the request for a human operator

explanation.correct: The agent runs the actual script referenced by the skill and relays that script's real output back to the user.
explanation.why: Answering from training data would risk hallucinated infra state; asking the user to open the console defeats the purpose of the skill; there's no human-operator queue in this design.

questions.question: Which AWS service is used to retrieve CPU utilization in this skill?

explanation.correct: CloudWatch's GetMetricStatistics call is what the skill uses to compute the CPU average.
explanation.why: Config tracks resource configuration compliance, not metrics; CloudTrail logs API calls, not performance metrics; X-Ray traces distributed application requests, not infrastructure CPU.

questions.question: What time window does the skill use for its CPU average?

explanation.correct: The skill reports CPU utilization as a 15-minute average pulled from CloudWatch.
explanation.why: 1 minute and 5 minutes are shorter windows than documented; 1 hour is longer than the 15-minute window actually used.

questions.question: Which tool runs the TypeScript script without a separate build step?

explanation.correct: tsx is named explicitly in the session and the repo as the TypeScript runner used to execute the script directly.
explanation.why: ts-node-dev, webpack, and babel are all real tools but none of them is the one documented for this specific skill.

questions.question: What credential type should NEVER be used to run this skill?

options.text: An IAM user with a read-only policy
Temporary credentials from an assumed role
An access key scoped to Describe/List/Get actions

explanation.correct: Root account credentials are explicitly warned against, since they grant unrestricted account access with no IAM boundary to limit the agent.
explanation.why: A read-only IAM user, assumed-role temporary credentials, and a scoped Describe/List/Get key are all acceptable or recommended credential types.

questions.question: Which environment variable is optional rather than required?

explanation.correct: AWS_SESSION_TOKEN is only needed for temporary or assumed-role credentials, while the other three are always required.
explanation.why: AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID, AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY, and AWS_REGION are all marked required in SKILL.md's documented environment variables.

questions.question: What command switches OpenClaw from the config agent to the main agent?

explanation.correct: "talk to agent" is the documented command that switches context into the main conversational agent where skills actually run.
explanation.why: gateway restart reloads config but doesn't switch agents; the tsx command runs the script directly, bypassing the agent entirely; there's no documented "openclaw login" command in this flow.

questions.question: After editing openclaw.json, what must you do for changes to take effect?

explanation.correct: "openclaw gateway restart" is explicitly required after any config change for it to be picked up.
explanation.why: Reinstalling Node.js and recreating the IAM user are unrelated to config reload; changes do not apply instantly without a restart.

questions.question: Which ECS API call sequence does the skill follow to build its report?

options.text: ListClusters, then ListServices, then DescribeServices, then ListTasks, then DescribeTasks

explanation.correct: This is the documented walk-through chain used to gather full ECS cluster, service, and task data.
explanation.why: RunTask/StopTask are mutating actions never used by a read-only skill; CreateService/UpdateService are also mutating actions; DescribeClusters alone wouldn't surface per-service or per-task detail.

questions.question: What does an EC2 "system status check" specifically detect?

options.text: Application-level errors in the guest OS

explanation.correct: System status checks catch hardware-layer problems with the underlying AWS infrastructure, distinct from instance/OS-level checks.
explanation.why: Guest-OS errors are covered by instance status checks instead; IAM issues surface as AccessDeniedException, not a status check; DNS failures aren't part of EC2 status checks at all.

questions.question: What does ECS "deployment rollout state" indicate?

options.text: The cost of the current deployment
Whether the deployment is COMPLETED, IN_PROGRESS, or FAILED
The number of available instance types
The region the cluster is deployed in

explanation.correct: These three values, COMPLETED, IN_PROGRESS, and FAILED, are the documented possible rollout states.
explanation.why: Cost isn't reported by DescribeServices; available instance types relate to EC2, not an ECS deployment; the region is set via configuration, not the rollout state field.

questions.question: Why does the skill report the last 5 stopped ECS tasks with stop reasons?

options.text: To calculate billing for stopped tasks
To help detect crash-loop patterns that a single snapshot would miss

explanation.correct: Repeated recent stops sharing the same reason reveal a crash loop that wouldn't be visible from a single point-in-time running count.
explanation.why: The skill doesn't compute billing; it never restarts tasks, since it's read-only by design; it also never deletes task definitions.

questions.question: Which IAM action prefixes are present in the skill's policy?

explanation.correct: Only the non-mutating Describe/List/Get action prefixes are granted, matching the skill's read-only design.
explanation.why: Create/Put/Delete, Terminate/Stop/Modify, and Attach/Detach/Update are all mutating action families excluded from this policy on purpose.

questions.question: Which error indicates the calling IAM identity lacks a required permission?

explanation.correct: AccessDeniedException maps directly to insufficient IAM permissions for the requested action.
explanation.why: CredentialsProviderError indicates missing/invalid credentials rather than a permission gap; ResourceNotFoundException means the resource doesn't exist; ThrottlingException relates to rate limits, not permissions.

questions.question: Which error would you see if you queried a non-existent ECS cluster name?

explanation.correct: A wrong or nonexistent cluster name maps to ResourceNotFoundException, as documented in the skill's error handling.
explanation.why: CredentialsProviderError relates to credentials, not cluster names; AccessDeniedException relates to permissions; ValidationException typically relates to malformed input rather than a missing resource.

questions.question: What flag scopes the health check to EC2 only?

explanation.correct: "--only ec2" is the exact documented flag for limiting the script to EC2 checks.
explanation.why: --ec2-only, --mode ec2, and --filter=ec2 are plausible-sounding but not the documented flag syntax used by this script.

questions.question: What flag scopes ECS checks to a single named cluster?

explanation.correct: "--only ecs --cluster my-cluster-name" is the exact documented invocation for scoping to one cluster.
explanation.why: The other three options use plausible but undocumented flag syntax not supported by this script.

questions.question: What is the relationship between {baseDir} in SKILL.md's invoke command and the skill folder?

options.text: {baseDir} is resolved by OpenClaw to the skill's own folder location
{baseDir} refers to the user's home directory
{baseDir} is unused and can be omitted

explanation.correct: {baseDir} is a placeholder OpenClaw substitutes at runtime with the path to the skill's own folder, making the command portable across installs.
explanation.why: It is not hardcoded, it isn't the home directory, and it isn't unused — it's essential to the cd command actually working.

questions.question: Why might multiple users sharing one OpenClaw instance be a security concern?

options.text: The model becomes less accurate with more users
Users may share the same file system, breaking user isolation

explanation.correct: Shared file system access across users can break isolation guarantees between sessions, which was raised as a core security concern in the session Q&A.
explanation.why: Billing isn't directly tied to sharing an instance; model accuracy isn't affected by the number of users; CloudWatch metrics are unrelated to this concern.

questions.question: What is the correct AWS SDK v3 action to add for RDS health checks, per the documented extension ideas?

explanation.correct: DescribeDBInstances is the action named in the session's extension roadmap for adding RDS health to the skill.
explanation.why: ListBuckets is an S3 action, DescribeLoadBalancers is an ELB action, and GetQueueAttributes is an SQS action — none relate to RDS.

questions.question: Which AWS API would extend the skill to report Application Load Balancer target health?

explanation.correct: DescribeTargetHealth is named explicitly as the ALB extension idea for reporting target group health.
explanation.why: DescribeInstances is an EC2 call; DescribeListeners reports ALB listener config, not target health; DescribeVpcs is unrelated to ALB health.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the AWS_SESSION_TOKEN environment variable when it is provided?

options.text: It replaces the access key entirely
It is required for temporary credentials from an assumed IAM role

explanation.correct: AWS_SESSION_TOKEN is documented as needed specifically when using temporary credentials obtained from an assumed role via STS.
explanation.why: It does not replace the access key, it's used alongside it; it has nothing to do with setting the region; it plays no role in encrypting chat messages.

questions.question: If the agent needed to push proactive alerts instead of waiting for a user query, which AWS service would be the natural trigger source, per the roadmap discussion?

explanation.correct: An SNS-triggered push to WhatsApp is the documented v2 idea for proactive alerting in this skill's roadmap.
explanation.why: Config Rules, Trusted Advisor, and Macie are all real AWS services but none was the one named for this specific proactive-alert extension.

questions.question: Why is using DescribeInstances/DescribeServices preferable to the AWS Health API for this specific use case?

options.text: The Health API is more expensive
The Health API only reports account/service-level status, not per-resource detail for a specific instance or service
The Health API cannot be called via the SDK

explanation.correct: The Health API lacks the per-resource granularity this skill needs, since it reports broader account- or service-level operational issues rather than the status of one specific instance or service.
explanation.why: Cost isn't the deciding factor here; the Health API can be called via the SDK; speed isn't the documented reason for the choice.

questions.question: What architectural property makes the agent structurally incapable of mutating infrastructure, independent of how the LLM behaves?

options.text: The script has a hardcoded confirmation prompt
The underlying IAM policy contains no mutating actions
AWS blocks all automated API calls by default
The script runs in a read-only Docker container

explanation.correct: The IAM policy itself is the enforcement boundary — since it contains no Create/Modify/Delete actions, the agent cannot mutate infrastructure regardless of what the LLM decides to do.
explanation.why: There is no hardcoded confirmation prompt described; AWS does not block all automated calls by default; the script isn't documented as running in a Docker container at all.

questions.question: In a multi-region extension (AWS_REGIONS=ap-south-1,us-east-1), what change would the script require at minimum?

options.text: None — the SDK clients automatically detect all regions
Instantiate one client per region and aggregate results
Switch from AWS SDK v3 back to v2
Replace EC2 calls with CloudFront calls

explanation.correct: AWS SDK clients are region-scoped, so a multi-region extension requires creating one client instance per region and combining the results into a single report.
explanation.why: SDK clients do not auto-detect multiple regions; there's no need to downgrade to SDK v2; CloudFront is unrelated to EC2/ECS health reporting.

questions.question: Why does the script print output in a structured, emoji-coded format rather than raw JSON?

options.text: JSON cannot be parsed by OpenClaw
The output is meant to be relayed directly to chat as-is, optimized for human chat readability
Emoji output is required by the AWS SDK

explanation.correct: SKILL.md instructs the agent to present the script's output as-is since it's already formatted for chat readability, removing any need for the agent to reformat raw JSON.
explanation.why: OpenClaw can parse JSON fine; the AWS SDK has no emoji requirement; output formatting has no bearing on CloudWatch cost.

questions.question: What's the most defensible reason to keep the agent and its skill scripts running locally rather than as a hosted cloud service?

options.text: It avoids the need for an inbound public endpoint and keeps credentials on infrastructure the operator directly controls
AWS SDK v3 cannot run in the cloud

explanation.correct: Running locally avoids exposing an inbound public endpoint and keeps the AWS credentials confined to infrastructure the operator directly controls, reducing the attack surface compared to a hosted service.
explanation.why: Local execution isn't inherently cheaper in all cases; AWS SDK v3 runs fine in the cloud; WhatsApp doesn't categorically block cloud-hosted bots.
